coredata.dc:title: Poly(bromocresol green)/carbon quantum dots modified electrode for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine
coredata.dc:creator: He, Shaoying
He, Ping
Zhang, Xingquan
Zhang, Xiaojuan
Li, Caixia
Dong, Faqin
Lei, Hong
Liu, Dechun
coredata.dc:description: A novel poly(bromocresol green) (PBG)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite based sensor was successfully fabricated on glassy carbon electrode by electropolymerization. As-prepared PBG/CQDs modified electrode was successfully applied in the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine. Electrochemical responses of as-prepared modified electrode were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry. Owing to the synergistic effect of PBG and CQDs, the oxidation peaks of both guanine and adenine well separated from each other and the peak potential separation was large up to 310mV. The current of the oxidation peaks showed a linear dependence on the concentrations of guanine and adenine in the range of 0.50–142 and 0.30–130μM with detection limits of 0.016 and 0.026μM (S/N=3), respectively. Moreover, as-prepared PBG/CQDs modified electrode exhibited favorable selectivity, stability and reproducibility, which had promising application for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(bromocresol green)
Carbon quantum dots
Modified electrode
Guanine
Adenine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs and as-prepared composite
Influence of electropolymerization on PBG/CQDs modified electrode
Electrocatalytic oxidation of guanine and adenine
Optimization of determination conditions of guanine and adenine
Effect of scan rate on as-prepared electrode
Electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine
Stability and reproducibility of as-prepared electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of modified electrode and application for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.
TEM image of CQDs (A), SEM images of GCE (B) and PBG/CQDs/GCE (C).
XRD pattern (A) and UV–Vis absorption spectrum (B) of CQDs.
ATR spectra of CQDs (a), PBG/GCE (b) and PBG/CQDs/GCE (c).
CVs of 40μM guanine and 40μM adenine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on GCE (a), CQDs/GCE (b), PBG/GCE (c) and PBG/CQDs/GCE (d), respectively. Scan rate: 50mV/s.

DPVs of 30μM guanine and 30μM adenine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer with pH2.7 (a), 3.7 (b), 4.7 (c) and 5.7 (d) on PBG/CQDs/GCE (A). Effect of pH on oxidation peak potentials (B) and oxidation peak currents (C) for guanine and adenine.

DPVs of guanine and adenine with different concentrations in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on PBG/CQDs/GCE (A). DPVs of guanine with different concentrations in the presence of 5.0μM adenine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on PBG/CQDs/GCE (B). DPVs of adenine with different concentrations in the presence of 5.0μM guanine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on PBG/CQDs/GCE (C). Insets were the linear calibration plots of currents versus concentrations of guanine and adenine, respectively. The error bars represented the standard deviations of three measurements.
Electrochemical oxidation mechanisms of guanine and adenine.
Comparison of different modified electrodes for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.
Simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine in fish sperm DNA on PBG/CQDs/GCE (n =3).

head.ce:title: Poly(bromocresol green)/carbon quantum dots modified electrode for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel poly(bromocresol green) (PBG)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite based sensor was successfully fabricated on glassy carbon electrode by electropolymerization. As-prepared PBG/CQDs modified electrode was successfully applied in the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine. Electrochemical responses of as-prepared modified electrode were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry. Owing to the synergistic effect of PBG and CQDs, the oxidation peaks of both guanine and adenine well separated from each other and the peak potential separation was large up to 310mV. The current of the oxidation peaks showed a linear dependence on the concentrations of guanine and adenine in the range of 0.50–142 and 0.30–130μM with detection limits of 0.016 and 0.026μM (S/N=3), respectively. Moreover, as-prepared PBG/CQDs modified electrode exhibited favorable selectivity, stability and reproducibility, which had promising application for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.

Shown up were preparation procedure and application of as-prepared electrode. Shown down were DPVs of the simultaneous determinations of guanine and adenine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on PBG/CQDs/GCE.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) plays an important role in life process which stores genetic information and controls protein biosynthesis [1]. Guanine and adenine are important purine components and coexist in DNA [2]. Their abnormal concentrations in the DNA transcription may cause many diseases [3]. Therefore, simple methods for simultaneous or individual determination of guanine and adenine are significant for clinical diagnosis. Up to now, some methods have been investigated for single or simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine, mainly including spectroscopy [4], chromatography [5] and electrochemistry [6]. Among these methods, electrochemical method used for single or simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine has attracted special attention because of its fast determination, low cost, easy operation and efficiency [7]. For example, Yao et al. used PBG modified electrode for the single determination of guanine [8]. Wei et al. developed a poly(eriochrome black T) modified electrode for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine [9].

As well known, dye is one kind of organics, which is used in weaving industry, pH indicator, tracking dye for electrophoresis and solar cell, etc. [10,11]. Moreover, dye can be turned into dye polymer by polymerization. Based on the properties of stability, biocompatibility and homogeneity, dye polymers play important roles in corrosion protection and sensor, such as poly(brilliant cresyl blue), poly(nile blue A), poly(bromocresol green) (PBG), poly(neutral red) and so on [12,13]. As one kind of dye polymers, PBG has favorable electrocatalysis. For example, Ouyang et al. used PBG for the simultaneous determination of uric acid, dopamine and ascorbic acid [14]. Ran et al. built PBG-Fe3O4 composite and achieved sensitive determination of serotonin [15]. By far, PBG based simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine has not been investigated.

Nowadays, nanostructured carbon materials have attracted great interest with respect to potential applications, such as supercapacitors [16], fuel cells [17], sensors [18] and adsorption [19]. Generally, carbon materials mainly include graphene, mesoporous carbon and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [20–24]. As a kind of nanostructured carbon materials, CQDs have some properties with good photoluminescence properties, low toxicity and efficient catalytic activity, etc. [25–27]. Because of these advantages, CQDs have obtained plenty of attention for potential applications in some fields of photocatalysis [28], sensors [29,30] and bioimaging [31], etc. Several CQDs based sensors have been reported. For instance, Campos et al. coated poly(amidoamine) with CQDs for determination of Pt (IV) [32]. Yang et al. developed a sensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on CQDs/graphene for determination of chlorinated phenols [33]. Li et al. investigated the electrochemical behavior of glucose and hydrogen peroxide based on CQDs/octahedral Cu2O nanocomposites modified electrode [34]. Hence, it would be interesting to utilize CQDs on modified electrodes for electrochemical sensors.

In this work, a novel electrochemical sensor based on PBG and CQDs has been developed towards the simultaneous electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine. Owing to the synergistic effect of PBG and CQDs, the peak potential separation of guanine-adenine reached up to 310mV, which was large enough to successfully realize the simultaneous electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine with favorable selectivity, stability and reproducibility.

Bromocresol green, citric acid (CA), K3[Fe(CN)6], KCl, NaOH, HAc, NaAc, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NH3·H2O and NH4Cl were purchased from Chengdu Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Guanine, adenine and fish sperm DNA were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All of the chemicals were analytical grade.

HAc-NaAc buffers (pH2.7, 3.7, 4.7 and 5.7) were prepared by mixing 0.10M HAc solution and 0.10M NaAc solution. Phosphate buffer (PBS, pH7.4) was prepared by mixing 0.10M NaH2PO4 solution and 0.10M Na2HPO4 solution. NH3-NH4Cl buffer (pH9.2) was prepared by mixing 0.10M NH3·H2O solution and 0.10M NH4Cl solution. The stock solutions of 0.10mM guanine and 0.10mM adenine were prepared daily and kept in an ice bath.

DNA sample was prepared as follows [35]. 10.0mg fish sperm DNA was digested using 2.0mL 1.0M HCl solution. After heating in boiling water bath for 60min, 2.0mL 1.0M NaOH was added. Subsequently the obtained solution was cooled in room temperature and was diluted to 50mL using 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7).
Highly pure nitrogen (99.999%) was used for deaeration in the electrochemical determination. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the whole experiment.

1.0g CA was heated and melted at 180°C. After 30min of heating, the melted CA liquid became orange, indicating the formation of CQDs [36]. Afterwards, the melted CA liquid was slowly added in NaOH solution (0.25M) with stirring and pH was adjusted to 7.0. The as-prepared CQDs were diluted to 200mL with doubly distilled water and refrigerated at 3°C.
Shown in Fig. 1 were the preparation of modified electrode and application for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.
Prior to modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 500 and 50nm alumina slurry and then washed successively with ethanol and doubly distilled water for several times.

Electrochemical deposition of PBG/CQDs on GCE was carried out by cyclic voltammetry (CV) from −1.0–1.8V with scan rate of 50mV/s for ten cycles in 0.10M NaOH solution containing 1.0mM bromocresol green and 1.0mg/mL CQDs. As-prepared electrode was thoroughly washed with doubly distilled water carefully and dried in air. For comparison, PBG/GCE and CQDs/GCE were prepared under the same conditions.
Before electrochemical measurements, the electrolytes were purged with N2 for 2min to remove oxygen completely.

The morphology of CQDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Libra 200FE, Carl Zeiss), and the morphologies GCE and PBG/CQDs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Ultra 55, Carl Zeiss). The structure of CQDs was investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X' Pert PRO, Netherlands) with Cu K α radiation (k =0.154060nm) and recorded from 3° to 60° at a speed of 2°/min. UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded by UV–Vis spectrometer (Specord 200PLUS, Analytik Jena AG). Attenuated total reflection (ATR) spectra of CQDs, PBG and PBG/CQDs were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet-5700, USA) with an ATR attachment in wavenumber range of 3500–1000cm−1.

CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were recorded with a PARSTAT 2273 electrochemical workstation (Princeton Applied Research, USA) by introducing a three-electrode test system using a platinum electrode as counter electrode, bare or modified GCE as working electrode referred to saturated calomel electrode (SCE). DPV parameters were as follows: increment potential of each point=0.004V, potential pulse amplitude=0.05V, potential pulse width=0.05s, data sampling width=0.0167s and potential pulse period=0.2s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and as-prepared composite

ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. 2A were TEM images of CQDs and amplified individual CQDs. Clearly, the shape of CQDs unit was spherical with a diameter about 7.6nm. Shown in Fig. 2B was SEM image of bare GCE. Compared with Fig. 2B, a coarse and homogeneous surface was observed on PBG/CQDs/GCE (Fig. 2C), which was fabricated by CV scans in the mixture solution containing bromocresol green and CQDs. The result proved that PBG/CQDs composite was successfully modified on GCE.

Shown in Fig. 3 were XRD pattern and UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs. Obviously, a broad peak of CQDs was observed at around 18.06°, which corresponded to the characteristic lattice plane (002) of CQDs [37] (Fig. 3A). Shown in Fig. 3B was UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs. Two broad peaks at 240 and 360nm were attributed to n-π* transition of CQDs [38].

Shown in Fig. 4 were ATR spectra of CQDs, PBG and PBG/CQDs. As observed in Fig. 4a, the band at 1450cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of –CH2, and the bands at 1725 and 1087cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of CO and CO of CQDs [32], respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the bands at 2850, 2920 and 1375cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of –CH3. The bands at 1634 and 1530cm−1 corresponded to benzene skeleton vibration, and the bands at 1041 and 1740cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of SO and CO of PBG [14], respectively. Compared with curves a and b, most absorption bands of PBG and CQDs were observed in PBG/CQDs (Fig. 4c), and the intensity of absorption peaks was much stronger than that of PBG or CQDs, indicating that PBG and CQDs were successfully composited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of electropolymerization on PBG/CQDs modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, polymer modified electrode can be prepared by electrochemical or chemical methods. In this work, PBG based modified electrode was prepared by electropolymerization method in 0.10M NaOH solution containing 1.0mM bromocresol green and 1.0mg/mL CQDs. Shown in Fig. S1 were CVs of 0.10M KCl solution containing 1.0mM K3[Fe(CN)6] on PBG/CQDs modified electrode prepared under different scan cycles (1–12cycles). Obviously, redox peak currents increased on PBG/CQDs modified electrode which was prepared under the number of scan cycles (1–10cycles), and then corresponding currents decreased when the preparation conditions of numbers of scan cycles were over 10cycles. It was demonstrated that 10cycles were used as optimum condition for the construction of PBG/CQDs modified electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic oxidation of guanine and adenine
ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. 5 were CVs of 40μM guanine and 40μM adenine in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) on GCE, CQDs/GCE, PBG/GCE and PBG/CQDs/GCE. As shown in Fig. 5a, there were two broad and weak peaks on bare GCE. Conversely, higher oxidation peak currents of guanine and adenine were observed in Fig. 5b, c and d. Compared with CQDs/GCE and PBG/GCE, the oxidation peak currents of guanine and adenine enhanced on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE. The oxidation peak potentials of guanine and adenine were located at 0.82 and 1.13V on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE, and the peak potential separation of guanine-adenine oxidation potential was 310mV.
Therefore, it was successful to achieve the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of determination conditions of guanine and adenine
ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. S2 were DPVs of 40μM guanine and 40μM adenine in different buffers of 0.10Μ HAc-NaAc (pH4.7), 0.10Μ PBS (pH7.4) and 0.10Μ NH3-NH4Cl (pH9.2) on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE, respectively. The potentials of oxidation peaks of both guanine and adenine shifted negatively with increasing pH, and the response signal was the largest in HAc-NaAc buffer than that in PBS or NH3-NH4Cl buffer. The results indicated that different buffers had obvious influences on oxidation peak potentials and currents of guanine and adenine.

pH of supporting electrolyte is an important factor that affects the electrochemical reaction of analyte [39]. The effect of pH on the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE was investigated in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer with pH in range of 2.7–5.7. As shown in Fig. 6A, the potentials of oxidation peaks of both guanine and adenine shifted negatively with increasing pH, indicating that protons were involved in the electrode reaction. Shown in Fig. 6B were the relationships between pH and oxidation peak potentials of guanine and adenine. The linear relationships were expressed by regression equations of E pa/V=(1.035±0.008)−(0.055±0.002) pH for guanine and E pa/V=(1.379±0.010)−(0.064±0.002) pH for adenine with correlation coefficients of 0.997 and 0.996, respectively. The slopes of guanine and adenine were close to Nernst value of 59mV/pH, indicating the number of electron transfer and proton transfer in equal amounts.

Electrochemical oxidations of G and A followed a two-step and a three-step mechanisms, respectively, and the rate-limiting step was the first step, which involved in a two-electron oxidation process (Scheme 1 ) [40].

Shown in Fig. 6C were the relationships between pH and oxidation peak currents of guanine and adenine. The oxidation peak currents of guanine and adenine reached up to the maximum at pH4.7. Summarily, HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) was selected for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on as-prepared electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. S3 were CVs of 0.10M KCl solution containing 1.0mM K3[Fe(CN)6] solution on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE with different scan rates. As shown in Fig. S3A, the redox peak currents increased with increasing scan rates. As shown in Fig. S3B, redox peak current showed a linear dependence on square root of the scan rate. It was demonstrated that the electrochemical process of K3[Fe(CN)6] on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE was diffusion-controlled [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of guanine and adenine
ce:section.ce:para: Shown in Fig. 87A were DPVs of guanine and adenine with different concentrations in the mixture on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE. As expected, two well-separated peaks were observed, and all of peak currents increased with increasing the concentrations of guanine and adenine. The result indicated that the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine was feasible in mixture solution of guanine and adenine. For further demonstrating the feasibility of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine, the individual determinations were also carried out. Guanine or adenine with different concentrations of in the mixture was investigated when the concentration of one species changed, while the other species remained constant.

Shown in Fig. 7B were the peak currents of guanine with increasing guanine concentration from 0.50–142μM. The linear regression equation was expressed as I pa/(μA)=−(0.367±0.043)−(0.072±0.0006)× c/(μM), with correlation coefficient of 0.993, and the detection limit was 0.016μM (S/N=3). Similarly, as observed in Fig. 7C, the peak currents of adenine increased linearly with increasing adenine concentration from 0.30–130μM. The linear regression equation was expressed as I pa/(μA)=−(0.008±0.051)−(0.047±0.007)× c/(μM), with correlation coefficient of 0.998, and the detection limit was 0.026μM (S/N=3).

Listed in Table 1 was a comparison of the as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE with other modified electrodes. It was found that as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE exhibited favorable linear range and detection limit for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine.

To evaluate the selectivity of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE, various potentially interfering substances were investigated in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) containing 40μM guanine and 40μM adenine and the following potential components (tolerance ratio): 100-fold of Cl−, NH4 +, NO3 −, 10-fold of glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid. It was found that no significant interference (signal changes below 5%) was observed, and the results indicated that as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE had high selectivity towards the determination of guanine and adenine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of as-prepared electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Stability and reproducibility of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE were evaluated for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine. The as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE was stored in 0.10M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH4.7) at 4°C in refrigerator when it was not used. The stability of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE was evaluated by measuring the current responses for the mixture of guanine and adenine over 2weeks. The current responses decreased by only 3.6% and 4.1% for 40μM guanine and 40μM adenine, respectively. Additionally, twenty times repeated measurements for guanine and adenine on as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE were carried out and relative standard deviations for both guanine and adenine were <5%, confirming that as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE had satisfactory reproducibility. These results indicated that the stability and reproducibility of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE were satisfactory.

The application of as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE was investigated by detecting guanine and adenine in fish sperm DNA with standard addition method. Different amounts of guanine and adenine were added into the sample solution to test recovery rates, and the results were shown in Table 2 . It was observed that the recovery rates of guanine and adenine were 99.0–102.0% and 99.5–102.3%, respectively. Therefore, recovery rates of sample analysis had satisfactory results.

In this work, PBG/CQDs composite modified electrode has been prepared by simple and rapid one-step electropolymerization method and used for the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine for the first time. Owing to the synergistic effect of PBG and CQDs, as-prepared PBG/CQDs/GCE exhibited favorable electrocatalysis, high selectivity, long-time stability and preferable reproducibility for the oxidation of guanine and adenine. Meanwhile, the proposed method was applied in the simultaneous determination of guanine and adenine in real biological samples with satisfactory results.

This work was supported by the Longshan Academic Talent Research Supporting Program of SWUST (17LZX406) and the National Basic Research Program of China (2014CB846003) and the National Science & Technology Supported Program (2014BAC13B05). Also we are grateful for the help of Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.10.069.
